title: Aloo gobi
subtitle: Dry-fried potato and cauliflower curry
excerpt: Potato and cauliflower florets cooked in a dry spice mixture of turmeric, cumin, coriander, and chilli until the edges are golden and the vegetables are coated in the caramelised spice crust. A North Indian dry curry with no sauce — the vegetables fry in the spiced oil rather than simmering in liquid. One of the great curry-house side dishes.
sourceNotes: Aloo gobi is a Punjabi dry curry, documented in Madhur Jaffrey's 'An Invitation to Indian Cooking' (1973) and widely reproduced in British-Indian cookbooks. It is one of the most common vegetarian dishes in British curry-houses and in South Asian home cooking.

content.text: Aloo gobi is a dry curry, which means the technique is closer to stir-frying than braising. The pan should be hot enough that the vegetables sizzle when added, and the lid should be kept on for the first 15 minutes to let them steam and cook through, then removed for the final stage to let the moisture evaporate and the spice crust form. The golden, slightly crisp edges on the potato and cauliflower pieces are the goal.

Cut the potatoes into 2 cm cubes, no larger, and the cauliflower into similar-sized florets. Uniform pieces cook evenly. Pre-cooking the potato for 5 minutes before adding the cauliflower is worth doing: potatoes take longer than cauliflower to soften, and adding them together can lead to the cauliflower over-softening while the potato catches up.

items.prepNote: peeled and cut into 2 cm cubes

content.text: Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide, heavy pan over medium-high heat. Add the {{cumin-seeds}} and fry for 30 seconds. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 8 minutes until golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{turmeric}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, and {{chilli-powder}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{potato}} pieces and toss to coat. Add a splash of water (about 4 tbsp), cover, and cook for 10 minutes.

Add the {{cauliflower}} florets, stir to coat, replace the lid, and cook for 8 to 10 minutes until both vegetables are tender. Remove the lid, increase the heat, and cook for a further 3 to 4 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the edges start to colour and any moisture evaporates. Season with salt, stir in the {{garam-masala}}, and scatter over fresh coriander to serve.

items.cause: Too much water added or lid on too long
items.fix: Use only a small splash of water for steaming. Remove the lid early and increase the heat to dry out the dish. Aloo gobi should have some texture — cauliflower should hold its shape

items.symptom: Cauliflower overcooked while potato is still firm
items.cause: Both added at the same time
items.fix: Cook the potato for 10 minutes before adding the cauliflower. They then finish cooking together

items.cause: Insufficient oil or heat too high without liquid
items.fix: Add a splash more oil if sticking. The splash of water under the lid at the potato stage helps prevent sticking at the dry stage

items.cause: Lid on the whole time or not enough heat for final stage
items.fix: The uncovered final stage at higher heat is where the golden edges develop. Don't skip it — this is the texture that makes the dish

content.text: Aloo gobi is the vegetarian benchmark of North Indian home cooking: the same spice foundation used for dozens of other dry curries, applied to the most pantry-standard vegetables. In British Indian homes it appears as a side dish alongside dal and rice, or as a filling for stuffed parathas. On the curry-house menu it is ordered as a side dish alongside meat curries, providing a contrast in texture and a lighter element to the meal. The dish crossed from home cooking to restaurant menu without changing its character significantly.
